text: If someone is drowning: do not jump in unless trained. Throw a rope, life ring, or any floating object. Once out of water, check for breathing. If not breathing, begin CPR — 30 chest compressions followed by 2 rescue breaths. Continue until the person breathes or emergency help arrives. Keep the person warm and on their side to prevent choking. Source: Philippine Red Cross First Aid Manual.

Floodwater is heavily contaminated. Do not drink it. Boil all water for at least 1 minute before drinking. Symptoms of leptospirosis include fever, headache, red eyes, and muscle pain — seek medical care immediately if these appear after flood exposure. For diarrhea and vomiting, drink oral rehydration solution (ORS) — 1 liter water, 6 teaspoons sugar, half teaspoon salt. Seek a doctor if symptoms persist beyond 24 hours. Source: DOH Philippines.

For bleeding wounds: apply firm direct pressure with a clean cloth for at least 10 minutes without lifting. Elevate the injured limb above heart level. Do not remove embedded objects — stabilize them. Clean minor wounds with clean water. Cover with a sterile bandage. Watch for signs of infection: increased pain, swelling, redness, warmth, or pus. Seek medical care for deep wounds, wounds that won't stop bleeding, or any puncture wound. Source: Philippine Red Cross First Aid Manual.

For burns: immediately cool the burn under cool (not ice cold) running water for at least 10-20 minutes. Do not use ice, butter, toothpaste, or any home remedy. Do not break blisters. Cover loosely with a clean non-fluffy material. For burns larger than the patient's palm, burns on the face, hands, feet, genitals, or joints — seek emergency medical care immediately. Chemical burns: flush with large amounts of water for 20 minutes. Source: Philippine Red Cross First Aid Manual.

Heat stroke is life-threatening. Signs: body temperature above 40°C, confusion, no sweating, rapid pulse. Act immediately: move person to shade or cool area, remove excess clothing, apply cool wet cloths to neck, armpits, groin. Fan them. Call 911. Do not give fluids to an unconscious person. Heat exhaustion (heavy sweating, weakness, cool pale skin) is less severe: rest in cool area, drink water slowly, loosen clothing. Source: DOH Philippines Heat Emergency Protocol.

If trapped in a collapsed structure: stay calm and conserve energy. Cover your mouth with a cloth to filter dust. Tap on a pipe or wall so rescuers can hear you. Shout only as a last resort to avoid inhaling dust. Do not light a match or lighter — gas lines may be ruptured. If possible, move to a doorway or under a sturdy table. Signal rescuers with a flashlight or whistle. Source: NDRRMC Family Emergency Preparedness Guide.

After a flood, do not eat food that has come into contact with floodwater. Discard canned goods with swollen lids, leaks, or dents along seams. Safe emergency foods: sealed commercially packaged items, fruits with thick skins (peeled before eating), cooked foods still hot. Purify water by boiling for 1 minute, or use water purification tablets. Store water in clean sealed containers. Source: DOH Philippines Disaster Food Safety Guidelines.

Infants and young children are at high risk during floods and heat emergencies. Keep infants dry and warm. During heat emergencies, never leave children in vehicles. For infants with fever above 38°C, seek medical care immediately. Breastfeeding is the safest option when clean water for formula is unavailable. Oral rehydration solution is safe for children over 2 months — consult a health worker for correct dosing. Source: DOH Philippines Child Health Emergency Guidelines.

Pregnant women should evacuate early — do not wait for a critical alert. If labor begins during evacuation: call 911 immediately, keep the mother calm and lying down, do not attempt home delivery unless absolutely unavoidable. Signs of emergency: heavy bleeding, severe abdominal pain, no fetal movement for 12+ hours, high fever. Seek the nearest hospital or health center with a birth facility. Source: DOH Philippines Maternal Emergency Protocol.

Elderly and persons with disabilities (PWD) must evacuate first. Bring all maintenance medications — at minimum 3 days supply. For mobility-impaired individuals, request barangay emergency assistance. During heat emergencies, elderly are at severe risk of heat stroke — ensure they have water and shade. Monitor for confusion or unresponsiveness which may indicate heat stroke or dehydration. Notify barangay health workers of any special medical needs on arrival at evacuation center. Source: NDRRMC.

During haze and smoke events: stay indoors with windows and doors closed. Use an N95 mask if you must go outside — a surgical mask does not filter fine particles. If you have asthma or a respiratory condition, keep your inhaler accessible and increase your medication as advised by your doctor. Symptoms requiring emergency care: difficulty breathing at rest, blue-tinged lips, chest pain, severe wheezing. Source: DOH Philippines Air Quality Health Advisory.

When ordered to evacuate: go immediately, do not wait. Take your go-bag: water (3 days), food (3 days), medications, important documents (IDs, land title, insurance), phone charger, flashlight, change of clothes, cash. Turn off electricity and gas before leaving. Do not drive through floodwater — 15cm of moving water can knock a person down, 30cm can sweep a vehicle. Proceed to your designated barangay evacuation center. Source: NDRRMC Family Emergency Preparedness Guide.
